chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum; we have its decompilation. It does ASN.1 parsing over a buffer pointer stored at param_1+0x10 and iterates using Compute_ASN1_ValueLength / LengthSize / ValueLength_from_LO, summing lengths until equals total length, returning the child count or 0 on error. No direct memcpy/memmove calls inside; just byte reads and calls to helper length functions.

Termination string is just the function name, not a signal; the exact crash type is unknown. However, the logic in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum includes a while loop that continues until iVar6 == total_length. Inside, it always reads one byte from the buffer before computing length size and value length, and it bails out (goto default) if it sees an unsupported tag or if ValueLength_from_LO returns -1. That default path sets the result to 0 and exits; there is no dereference of invalid pointers beyond the earlier buffer byte read, which is always checked by loop condition using iVar2 (total length). So as long as Compute_ASN1_ValueLength() returns a correct total length, there is no obvious out-of-bounds read here.

We do not see Compute_ASN1_ValueLength() body, but param_1 is an ASN.1 node object, not directly controlled; its buffer pointer is *(long*)(param_1+0x10). In Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, param_1 is a DER node obtained from Get_DER_Child and similar helper functions. Those helpers encapsulate DER structure navigation; fuzzing is likely injecting arbitrary ASN.1 bytes into a top-level CERT_Load path. Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is used as a validator (child count) before accessing specific children. It does not allocate or copy; so even if miscounts, the worst that happens in this function is returning an incorrect child count, which higher layers often check (e.g., require ==2, >=8, etc.) and bail out.

Given the termination label, it could be some internal sanity check / assert inside Compute_ASN1_ValueLength or other helpers that aborts when structure invariant fails under malformed fuzz inputs. That is a robustness bug but in a certificate-parsing routine, malformed certificates causing local crash are a potential DoS if unhandled. However, without evidence of memory corruption (no memcpy, no pointer arithmetic beyond bounded index), this looks like a safe parse error path under extreme inputs rather than an exploitable memory bug.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo likely takes a certificate blob from Java and calls CERT_Load then Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and TBS; those in turn call Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. For realistic apps, input certs typically come from the OS keystore, server TLS handshakes, or files; malformed data could exist but triggering a hard abort in this helper still yields at most a controlled crash/DoS. There are validation checks on child counts and null returns throughout Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, indicating defensive coding; no obvious unbounded operations on attacker-controlled lengths are present in the visible code.

Because the exact cause of termination is unknown and we have no indications of heap overflows, UAF, or out-of-bounds reads/writes in this frame, plus the function mainly performs length-accumulation and early bailout on errors, the evidence for a true memory-safety vulnerability is weak. It is more plausibly a harness-triggered abort due to feeding arbitrary bytes into a strict ASN.1 parser that is not designed to gracefully handle all malformed structures in this fuzz setup.
reasons: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum performs bounded iteration over an ASN.1 buffer using a total-length value and exits early on invalid tags/lengths; no unsafe memcpy/memmove or writes are visible
Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT checks child counts, nulls, and conversion results before use, suggesting failures propagate as parse errors rather than memory corruption
Crash reason is labeled only by function name without SIGSEGV/ASAN info, consistent with an internal abort/assert under malformed ASN.1 from fuzzing (likely local DoS at most)
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
recommendations: Instrument runs with ASan/UBSan to confirm absence of memory safety issues in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 helpers
Add robust error handling/return codes instead of any abort/assert paths in ASN.1 parsing so malformed certificates cannot crash the process
Perform additional manual review of Compute_ASN1_ValueLength / ValueLength_from_LO and Get_DER_Child for potential length miscalculation or bounds issues
assumptions: Compute_ASN1_ValueLength and Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO correctly enforce buffer bounds based on internal ASN.1 node length metadata
Crash is not accompanied by external reports (e.g., ASan logs) indicating heap corruption or out-of-bounds access
limitations: Body of Compute_ASN1_ValueLength / Compute_ASN1_LengthSize / Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO and Get_DER_Child not inspected here
Exact process termination signal or message is unknown; only function name reported

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. Need to inspect memcpy arguments there. Decompilation shows memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size) where __dest is malloc(__size), so destination size equals length, no overflow there if malloc succeeds. Source is param_1 + iVar10 and length __size is derived from ASN.1 length fields taken from param_1, which is attacker-controlled DER buffer coming from JNI path via BINSTR_SetData and CERT_Load into Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. If the declared length __size exceeds actual available bytes in param_1, memcpy will read out-of-bounds from param_1, causing crash in __memcpy. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST does not receive an explicit size parameter; it assumes param_1 points to a well-formed, sufficiently long DER blob. There is no bounds check against an input length, so malformed DER with overlarge length encodings can trigger OOB read. From JNI side, Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo obtains iVar4 = env->GetArrayLength(param_3) (or similar), allocates __s = malloc(sVar10), then unconditionally memcpy(__s, __src, sVar10). That part is safe as long as sVar10 matches Java array length, which it does. Then BINSTR_SetData(__s, iVar4, &local_14b8) wraps this into a BINSTR. CERT_Load(&local_14c0, &local_14b8) calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST(*param_2) where *param_2 is presumably pointer to BINSTR internal buffer. However Decode_DER_ASN1_ST signature only takes a pointer and never a length. CERT_Load passes only the pointer, so Decode_DER_ASN1_ST has no way to detect total buffer size. Therefore, malformed DER where the internal length field claims more data than present in the BINSTR will cause memcpy to read past end of BINSTR memory into adjacent heap, invoking undefined behaviour and potential information disclosure or crash. Crash in __memcpy corroborates this.

Need to confirm whether BINSTR layer might append a terminator or other padding that guarantees enough bytes. But even if BINSTR length equals Java array length, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST never reads that length; function prototype is only pointer. There are no internal guards on __size except integer range but nothing compares to actual allocated size. Thus this is a classical unchecked length field issue in a DER parser, i.e., out-of-bounds read based on attacker-controlled length.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input suggests input is arbitrary and under attacker control. In real app, jCertInfo is likely called with certificate data received from external sources (e.g., server or smart card). That is still attacker-influenced and should be considered untrusted. Therefore the vulnerability is realistic and reachable. The impact is at least high (OOB read), possibly higher depending on allocator behaviour, but we see only memcpy into newly malloc'd buffer, so it is an over-read of source, not write overflow. So CWE is OOB read (CWE-125) rather than write. Nonetheless, it enables crashes and potential info leakage.
Thus, classify as vulnerable, high severity, with confidence ~0.9 since code clearly lacks input size checks in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and derives memcpy length from untrusted ASN.1 contents.
reasons: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST calls memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size) where __size is derived from ASN.1 length fields inside param_1 with no bounds check against real buffer size.
JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo ultimately passes attacker-controlled certificate bytes into CERT_Load → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which only takes a pointer and has no length parameter.
Malformed DER with overlarge length values will cause memcpy to read past end of allocated buffer (out-of-bounds read), matching the observed crash in __memcpy.
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo -> BINSTR_SetData -> CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> memcpy
recommendations: Change Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (and callers) to accept both pointer and length, and validate ASN.1 length fields do not exceed remaining buffer bytes before memcpy.
Add robust DER parsing and error handling that aborts on inconsistent or oversized length encodings instead of trusting them.
Add fuzzing and ASan/UBSan builds for the ASN.1/DER handling code to catch similar length-handling bugs.
assumptions: param_1 in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST points to a buffer of size equal to the Java array length or BINSTR size, with no extra hidden padding.
jCertInfo is callable with untrusted or semi-trusted certificate data from external sources, not only fixed internal constants.
limitations: Internal implementation of BINSTR_* helpers is not inspected; analysis assumes they do not over-allocate large safety margins.
Exact root cause instruction inside __memcpy is not shown, so classification is based on static reasoning from decompiled code.

chain_of_thought: Top crashing frame is __memcpy, first app-level frame below it is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, which I have decompiled via MCP. I must inspect memcpy arguments there and trace their origin. In Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, after ASN.1 length parsing, it allocates __dest = malloc(__size); and then calls memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size). Here, dst = __dest (heap buffer of size __size), src = param_1 + iVar10 (pointer into caller-supplied DER data), len = __size. This memcpy is internally consistent because the destination buffer was allocated with exactly __size bytes. There is no immediate overflow of the allocated buffer. Potential problems at this memcpy could be: (1) __size extremely large (leading to malloc failure, but checked), (2) src pointer invalid/unmapped (if caller passes bogus param_1), or (3) integer issues in computing iVar10 or __size that could cause reading past the caller-provided region. I need to see how param_1 and the BINSTR container are controlled from higher layers to determine if this is a real vulnerability or just the harness providing an invalid pointer.

CERT_Load simply wraps Decode_DER_ASN1_ST: it takes param_2, dereferences *param_2 as the input buffer to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, and param_1 as output cert. It does no validation of the contents, but that is fine; Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is intended to parse arbitrary DER. The critical question is how *param_2 is set in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo.

In Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo, param_1 is a JNI environment/this pointer pair (actually a structure with function table), param_3 is likely a jbyteArray or similar certificate blob from Java/fuzzer. The code does: (1) iVar4 = (**(code **)(*param_1 + 0x558))(param_1, param_3); this is almost certainly GetArrayLength(env, jbyteArray). So sVar10 = (size_t)iVar4 is the byte-array length. (2) __src = (void *)(**(code **)(*param_1 + 0x5c0))(param_1,param_3,0); this is likely GetByteArrayElements or GetByteArrayRegion, returning a valid pointer to an array of length sVar10. (3) __s = malloc(sVar10); memset(__s,0,sVar10); memcpy(__s,__src,sVar10); So __s is a heap buffer of size sVar10 that copies all input bytes. Then (4) BINSTR_Create(&local_14b8); BINSTR_SetData(__s, iVar4, &local_14b8); CERT_Load(&local_14c0, &local_14b8).

I do not see the definition of BINSTR_Create/SetData, but from usage CERT_Load expects param_2 to be a binary-string container whose first element *param_2 points to the data buffer. In CERT_Load, it calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST(*param_2). So *param_2 should be the __s buffer and iVar4 the length. Provided BINSTR_SetData just stores the pointer and length, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST sees param_1 as a valid buffer of length sVar10 bytes controlled by the attacker (certificate bytes).

Within Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the ASN.1 length parser handles both short-form and long-form lengths. For long form, it uses param_1[1] & 0x7f as the number of length bytes, loops over them to build uVar7 = decoded length (unsigned), and checks if (int)uVar7 < 0 then goto LAB_0011780c (error path). However, there is no check that uVar7 (the decoded content length) plus header offset iVar10 fits within the original buffer length sVar10. It then sets __size = (size_t)(int)uVar7, allocates __dest = malloc(__size), and calls memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size). Thus, the memcpy itself writes within its own heap allocation, but it may read past the end of the original certificate buffer if the length encoded in ASN.1 claims more bytes than actually provided by the caller/fuzzer. That would cause an out-of-bounds read from param_1 into unmapped memory, leading to a crash at __memcpy. This is classic insufficient bounds checking on a parsed length field derived from untrusted data.

The attacker controls param_1 bytes via the Java jbyteArray. There is no validation of DER length before memcpy, and no usage of the known total buffer length sVar10 from JNI; Decode_DER_ASN1_ST receives only a pointer, not the size, so it cannot check. As a result, a crafted DER with a long-form length specifying a huge value causes malloc to attempt a large allocation; if malloc succeeds for some moderately large size smaller than address space but larger than the actual input, memcpy will perform an over-read from param_1 and may segfault or leak data if mapped. Even if malloc fails, code checks __dest != NULL and otherwise goes to Delete_ASN1 and returns 0, so the crash is specifically from memcpy reading past end of param_1. That is memory safety (out-of-bounds read) with attacker-controlled length and thus a vulnerability.

I consider whether this might be harness-only: the fuzzer may be handing a pointer that is not a valid DER or not even a valid jbyteArray. But in JNI, env functions for byte arrays are standard; libJVM ensures __src and length are consistent. The fuzz harness likely calls the JNI method with random byte arrays, which is realistic: in production the app will call jCertInfo with actual certificate blobs read from NFC/smartcard/server. An attacker able to control certificate data (for example, a malicious server or smartcard) can supply malformed DER. Thus, the bug is reachable in realistic use cases and not limited to the harness. It is a denial-of-service via crash and possible information leak or more subtle memory safety, though here it is a read overrun (not write) so RCE is less trivial but still potentially exploitable depending on platform protections.

The immediate memcpy in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo that builds local_1008 also uses attacker-controlled lengths (derived from strlen of local buffers) but those are derived from outputs of GetUser/GetIssuer etc., not directly from untrusted data, and those buffers are fixed-size local arrays (1104, 4096, etc.). Those memcpys mask size using & 0xff on length (one-byte length). The arrays are much larger than 255, so they are safe. The problematic memcpy is in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST because there is no view of original input length.

Therefore, the root cause is in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST: using an ASN.1 length value to drive memcpy without bounds checking against the actual buffer size. This is a classic out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) and possibly buffer overread (CWE-126); because memcpy source is attacker-controlled, it can lead to a crash (DoS) and possibly read of adjacent memory when the buffer belongs to a larger mapping. Severity: medium to high; it is memory safety but appears to be only out-of-bounds read. Absent further evidence of information exfiltration, I'll classify as high if used in a security-sensitive cert parser (could be used for side-channel or info leak), but conservatively medium-high. I will label severity as high because it is in certificate parsing of untrusted data in a banking app and may be triggered remotely, even if it is a read side only.
Call sequence entrypoint→JNI→CERT_Load→Decode_DER_ASN1_ST→__memcpy is clear from stack trace and decompiled code, so I can fill call_sequence accordingly.
reasons: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST parses ASN.1 length from attacker-controlled certificate bytes without tracking the overall buffer size
It allocates a buffer of size __size (decoded length) then calls memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size) without checking that iVar10 + __size <= original input length
JNI layer provides param_1 data directly from untrusted jbyteArray with no validation, so a crafted DER can cause out-of-bounds read in memcpy leading to crash
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo -> BINSTR_SetData -> CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> memcpy
recommendations: Modify Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (or BINSTR layer) to track and pass the total buffer length alongside the pointer, and verify that header offset + decoded length does not exceed this size before calling memcpy
Add robust DER/ASN.1 validation and length checks before parsing, rejecting inputs with excessive or inconsistent length fields
Include negative tests in unit/fuzz testing specifically targeting overlong and malformed ASN.1 length encodings
assumptions: BINSTR_SetData stores the __s pointer and its length and CERT_Load passes that pointer to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST
JNI env function pointers at offsets 0x558 and 0x5c0 are standard GetArrayLength/GetByteArrayElements semantics
In production, certificate bytes can be attacker-controlled (e.g., from smartcard or remote server)
limitations: Implementation details of BINSTR_Create/BINSTR_SetData not decompiled here; assumed conventional behavior
No direct view of fuzz harness implementation, but stack trace shows realistic JNI usage
